text: The surgical team uses four trochars for the procedure, with the patient positioned on their left side. The ileocolic anastomosis is identified, followed by a peritumoral injection of two milliliters of indocyanin green.

The surgeon conducts a lateral to medial approach due to the absence of the heliocolic pedicle, initially sectioning the lateral lesion. The main landmarks, including the duodenum and right ureter, are identified during the heliocolic resection.

Twenty-five minutes after the indocyanin green injection, the surgeon verifies the medial limit of resection. The superior mesenteric vein and some ileal branches are identified within the surgical field.

The surgeon isolates the small intestine approximately fifteen centimeters from the previous ileocolic anastomosis. A mesocolic excision is performed, following the superior mesenteric vein in a caudal to cranial direction, extending towards the pancreatic head. Fluorescence guidance is used to ensure the removal of all fluorescent lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon dissects the middle colic vessels that were preserved during the previous right colectomy, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs the ileocolic anastomosis using a stapler after confirming adequate vascularization of the intestinal stumps with an intraoperative intravenous injection of indocyanin green (ICG). Hemostasis is verified on the intraluminal side of the anastomosis, and the service bridge is sutured using a V-lock suture.
